meta.title: What is an occlusal scan? — Vincor Knowledge Base
meta.description: Everything about the DIERS 4D Spinescan: how the occlusal scan works, what it measures, and why it is safer and more accurate than traditional diagnostics.

body.text: At Vincor, the occlusal scan forms the starting point of every treatment. But what exactly is such a scan? What is measured? And how does it differ from an X-ray? In this article, we explain everything clearly.
The DIERS Formetric 4D Spinescan maps your full posture. Specifically, the system measures:

items: The position of the spine: curvature, rotation, and any scoliosis
The position of the pelvis: tilt, rotation, and lateral deviation
Muscle balance: symmetry of the core musculature on the left and right
Body statics: the overall balance of your body in three dimensions

body.text: With an occlusal scan, we perform this measurement twice: once in your current situation and once with a therapeutic test bite. By analyzing the difference, it becomes immediately visible whether and how much your bite affects your posture.
How does it work? Video-Raster-Stereography explained

The technology behind the scan is called Video-Raster-Stereography (VRS). The principle is surprisingly elegant: a projector casts a grid of light lines onto your back. Two cameras record how this light pattern is distorted by the contours of your body. A computer calculates from this a three-dimensional model of your back surface in fractions of a second.
Because the system works in 4D (three dimensions plus time), multiple images are captured per second. Thus, not only your posture is measured, but also the dynamics — how your body moves and sways.
The DIERS scan differs fundamentally from traditional diagnostics:

items: No radiation: the scan works exclusively with visible light. There is no X-ray radiation, no electromagnetic radiation, and no contact with the body.
Contactless: no sensors, markers, or stickers are placed on your skin.
Super fast: the measurement literally takes seconds. Including preparation, you are ready within a quarter of an hour.
Repeatable: because the scan is completely safe, it can be repeated as often as needed to measure progress.

body.text: The accuracy of the measurement is comparable to that of X-ray images — but without the disadvantages. This also makes the scan suitable for children, pregnant women, and patients who need to be monitored regularly.
What can you expect during the scan?

The process is simple and comfortable. You are asked to expose your upper body (women may wear an open-back shirt) and stand relaxed in front of the device. The system projects a pattern of light lines onto your back — you feel nothing of it.

After a few seconds, the first measurement is complete. Next, you receive a therapeutic test bite (a wax roll or silicone build-up) and the measurement is repeated. The results are immediately available and are discussed with you on the screen.
From scan results to treatment plan

The scan results form the basis for your personal treatment plan. The color maps show at a glance where your body is out of balance and how large the difference is with and without the test bite. Based on this, we determine which treatment is most suitable — often starting with a therapeutic occlusal splint.
Want to know more about the technology and diagnostics? Visit our comprehensive diagnostics page.
